title: Introduction to Docker
text:
Docker Command Line Interface (CLI) Syntax






Docker Command Line Interface (CLI) Syntax


This lesson uses the newer grouped Docker CLI syntax introduced with the Docker Engine version 1.13:

docker [command] [subcommand] [options]
common groups include image and container
this form is clearer than the older flat command style

Comparing the help outputs shows that Docker supports both old and newer CLI forms.

Older form: docker run
Newer grouped form: docker container run

The grouped form is more descriptive and is the one used in this workshop.

title: Introduction to Docker
text:
Challenge




Exploring a Command


Run docker --help and pick a command from the list. Explore the help prompt for that command. Try to guess how a command would work by looking at the Usage: section of the prompt.

title: Introduction to Docker
text:
Solution




Solution


Suppose we pick the docker image build command:
docker image build --help

Usage:  docker image build [OPTIONS] PATH | URL | -

Build an image from a Dockerfile

Options:
     --add-host list           Add a custom host-to-IP mapping (host:ip)
     --build-arg list          Set build-time variables
     --cache-from strings      Images to consider as cache sources
     --cgroup-parent string    Optional parent cgroup for the container
     --compress                Compress the build context using gzip
     --cpu-period int          Limit the CPU CFS (Completely Fair Scheduler) period
     --cpu-quota int           Limit the CPU CFS (Completely Fair Scheduler) quota
 -c, --cpu-shares int          CPU shares (relative weight)
     --cpuset-cpus string      CPUs in which to allow execution (0-3, 0,1)
     --cpuset-mems string      MEMs in which to allow execution (0-3, 0,1)
     --disable-content-trust   Skip image verification (default true)
 -f, --file string             Name of the Dockerfile (Default is 'PATH/Dockerfile')
     --force-rm                Always remove intermediate containers
     --iidfile string          Write the image ID to the file
     --isolation string        Container isolation technology
     --label list              Set metadata for an image
 -m, --memory bytes            Memory limit
     --memory-swap bytes       Swap limit equal to memory plus swap: '-1' to enable unlimited swap
     --network string          Set the networking mode for the RUN instructions during build (default "default")
     --no-cache                Do not use cache when building the image
     --pull                    Always attempt to pull a newer version of the image
 -q, --quiet                   Suppress the build output and print image ID on success
     --rm                      Remove intermediate containers after a successful build (default true)
     --security-opt strings    Security options
     --shm-size bytes          Size of /dev/shm
 -t, --tag list                Name and optionally a tag in the 'name:tag' format
     --target string           Set the target build stage to build.
     --ulimit ulimit           Ulimit options (default [])

We could try to guess that the command could be run like this:
docker image build .
docker image build https://github.com/docker/rootfs.git
Where https://github.com/docker/rootfs.git could be any relevant URL that supports a Docker image.

title: Introduction to Docker
text:
Container Terminology






Reminder of Terminology: Container Images and Containers



A container image is the reusable template.
A container is a running instance created from that image.

title: Introduction to Docker
text:
Downloading Docker Images
Start with a simple example image from Docker Hub: hello-world.
Use docker image commands to work with container images on your machine. Check what images are already available (“ls” is short for “list”):
docker image ls
On a fresh Docker install, this list is usually empty.
Pull the hello-world image from Docker Hub:
docker image pull hello-world
Expected output:
Using default tag: latest
latest: Pulling from library/hello-world
1b930d010525: Pull complete
Digest: sha256:f9dfddf63636d84ef479d645ab5885156ae030f611a56f3a7ac7f2fdd86d7e4e
Status: Downloaded newer image for hello-world:latest
docker.io/library/hello-world:latest






Docker Hub



hello-world comes from Docker Hub.
Docker Hub is an online registry for sharing container images.
We cover it in more detail later.

title: Introduction to Docker
text:
Exercise: Check on Your Images




Exercise: Check on Your Images


What command would you use to see if the hello-world Docker container image had downloaded successfully and was on your computer? Give it a try before checking the solution.

title: Introduction to Docker
text:
Verifying Downloaded Images




Solution


To see if the hello-world container image is now on your computer, run:
docker image ls





The downloaded hello-world image is not saved in your current folder.
Running ls will not show container images.
Docker stores images in its own internal storage location.
Use docker image commands to list and manage images on your computer.

title: Introduction to Docker
text:
Running the hello-world Container
Use docker container run to create and run a container from an image.

Syntax: docker container run IMAGE_NAME
Your current working directory does not matter for this example.

docker container run hello-world
Hello from Docker!
This message shows that your installation appears to be working correctly.

To generate this message, Docker took the following steps:
 1. The Docker client contacted the Docker daemon.
 2. The Docker daemon pulled the "hello-world" image from the Docker Hub.
    (amd64)
 3. The Docker daemon created a new container from that image which runs the
    executable that produces the output you are currently reading.
 4. The Docker daemon streamed that output to the Docker client, which sent it
    to your terminal.

To try something more ambitious, you can run an Ubuntu container with:
 `docker run -it ubuntu bash`

Share images, automate workflows, and more with a free Docker ID:
 https://hub.docker.com/

For more examples and ideas, visit:
 https://docs.docker.com/get-started/

title: Introduction to Docker
text:
Running the hello-world Container
docker container run does three things:

Starts a running container from the chosen image. Think of this as the “alive” or “inflated” version of the container.
Executes the image’s default command. This could be as simple as printing a message (as above) or running a whole analysis pipeline!
Once the default action is complete, the container stops running (or exits). The container image is still there, but nothing is actively running.

For hello-world, the default command prints this diagnostic message and exits.






Using docker container run to Get the Image



You can skip docker image pull.
If the image is missing, docker container run will pull it automatically and then run it.

title: Introduction to Docker
section: Running a Container with a Chosen Command
text:
Running a Container with a Chosen Command
Want to do more than the default container action?

Use a different image to explore more docker container run patterns.
The previous output suggested ubuntu.
Here we use alpine instead because it downloads faster.





Run the Alpine Docker Container


Try downloading the alpine container image and using it to run a container. You can do it in two steps, or one. What are they?

title: Introduction to Docker
text:
Run the Alpine Docker Container
What happened when you ran the Alpine Docker container?
docker container run alpine
What to expect:

First run: Docker may pull alpine if it is not already local.
Later runs: you may see little or no output.
Reason: alpine is minimal and usually expects you to provide a command.

Try running an explicit command:
docker container run alpine cat /etc/os-release
You should see Alpine OS details from /etc/os-release (version and related metadata).

title: Introduction to Docker
text:
Challenge




Hello World, Part 2


Can you run a copy of the alpine container and make it print a “hello world” message?
Give it a try before checking the solution.

title: Introduction to Docker
text:
Solution




Solution


Use the same command as above, but with the echo command to print a message.
docker container run alpine echo 'Hello World'




We can provide commands at the end of the docker container run command and they will execute inside the running container.

title: Introduction to Docker
text:
Running Containers Interactively
To run interactively,

add the interactive flags -i and -t (usually combined as -it)
provide a shell (bash, sh, etc.) as the command
For alpine, use sh because bash is not included.

docker container run -it alpine sh






Technically…



-i keeps standard input open.
-t allocates a text terminal (pseudo-TTY).
In practice, -it is the usual combination for interactive shells.

title: Introduction to Docker
text:
Running Containers Interactively
Your prompt should change to look like this:
/ #
You’re now inside the running container! Try these commands:

pwd
ls
whoami
echo $PATH
cat /etc/os-release
These commands run inside the container, not on your host computer.
The output describes the container environment.
When finished, exit the container with exit.

/ # exit

title: Introduction to Docker
text:
Challenge




Practice Makes Perfect


Can you find out the version of Ubuntu installed on the ubuntu container image? (Hint: You can use the same command as used to find the version of alpine.)
Can you also find the apt-get program? What does it do? (Hint: try passing --help to almost any command will give you more information.)

title: Introduction to Docker
text:
Solution




Solution 1 – Interactive


Run an interactive ubuntu container – you can use docker image pull first, or just run it with this command:
docker container run -it ubuntu sh
OR you can get the bash shell instead
docker container run -it ubuntu bash
Then try, running these commands
/# cat /etc/os-release
/# apt-get --help
Exit when you’re done.
/# exit








Solution 2 – Run Commands


Run a ubuntu container, first with a command to read out the Linux version:
docker container run ubuntu cat /etc/os-release
Then run a container with a command to print out the apt-get help:
docker container run ubuntu apt-get --help

title: Introduction to Docker
text:
Removing Images
Containers and images can accumulate and consume disk space over time.
To remove an image, identify it first, usually by Image ID:
docker image ls
REPOSITORY       TAG         IMAGE ID       CREATED          SIZE
hello-world      latest      fce289e99eb9   15 months ago    1.84kB
Remove by image ID:
docker image rm fce289e99eb9
Or remove by image name:
docker image rm hello-world
You may see this error:
Error response from daemon: conflict: unable to remove repository reference "hello-world" (must force) - 
container e7d3b76b00f4 is using its referenced image fce289e99eb9
Why this happens:

A container (running or stopped) is still linked to that image.
Remove relevant containers first, then remove the image.

title: Introduction to Docker
text:
What Containers Are Running?
Use docker container commands to inspect container state.
List currently running containers:
docker container ls
CONTAINER ID        IMAGE               COMMAND             CREATED             STATUS              PORTS               NAMES
No rows means no containers are currently running.






docker ps



docker ps is equivalent to docker container ls.
The name comes from the Unix ps command which describes running processes.

title: Introduction to Docker
text:
What Containers Have Run Recently?
To include stopped containers, add --all (or -a):
docker container ls --all
CONTAINER ID        IMAGE               COMMAND             CREATED             STATUS                     PORTS               NAMES
9c698655416a        hello-world         "/hello"            2 minutes ago       Exited (0) 2 minutes ago                       zen_dubinsky
6dd822cf6ca9        hello-world         "/hello"            3 minutes ago       Exited (0) 3 minutes ago                       eager_engelbart






Keeping It Clean



Docker keeps stopped containers unless you remove them.
Add --rm to delete the container automatically when it exits.
Do not use --rm if you need to inspect or reconnect to that container later.

title: Introduction to Docker
section: How Do I Remove an Exited Container?
text:
How Do I Remove an Exited Container?
Delete one exited container by ID:

Replace with the container ID you want to remove.
Docker echoes the ID on success.

docker container rm 9c698655416a
9c698655416a
Use docker container prune to delete all stopped containers.

It does not take a container ID.
Deletion is permanent.
Avoid this command if you may need to reconnect to stopped containers.
Docker asks for confirmation and then lists removed container IDs.

docker container prune
WARNING! This will remove all stopped containers.
Are you sure you want to continue? [y/N] y
Deleted Containers:
9c698655416a848278d16bb1352b97e72b7ea85884bff8f106877afe0210acfc
6dd822cf6ca92f3040eaecbd26ad2af63595f30bb7e7a20eacf4554f6ccc9b2b

title: Introduction to Docker
section: Removing Images, for Real This Time
text:
Removing Images, for Real This Time
After removing dependent containers, retry image removal:
docker image rm hello-world
Untagged: hello-world:latest
Untagged: hello-world@sha256:5f179596a7335398b805f036f7e8561b6f0e32cd30a32f5e19d17a3cda6cc33d
Deleted: sha256:fce289e99eb9bca977dae136fbe2a82b6b7d4c372474c9235adc1741675f587e
Deleted: sha256:af0b15c8625bb1938f1d7b17081031f649fd14e6b233688eea3c5483994a66a3
Why multiple lines appear:

Docker images are made of layered filesystems.
Removing one image may untag and/or delete several underlying layers.
Shared layers are kept until no image depends on them.

Finally, confirm hello-world is gone from docker image ls.

title: Introduction to Docker
text:
Introducing the Docker Hub
Docker Hub is an online registry of container images.

Many images are public.
Many are maintained by software developers.
Some are official/endorsed images with stronger trust signals.
Trusted images are more likely to be maintained, stable, and secure.







Docker Can Be Used without Connecting to Docker Hub



Docker Hub is convenient, but not required.
Some organizations use private or offline container infrastructure instead.

title: Introduction to Docker
section: Exploring an Example Docker Hub Page
text:
Exploring an Example Docker Hub Page
Use the official Python image page as an example: https://hub.docker.com/_/python
What to look for on a Docker Hub page:

Top-left: image name, short description, popularity, endorsements.
Top-right: pull command.
Main body sections typically include:

Which tags (i.e., container image versions) are supported;
Summary information about where to get help, which computer architectures are supported, etc.;
A longer description of the container image;
Examples of how to use the container image; and
The license that applies.


The “How to use this image” section is usually the best starting point.

title: Introduction to Docker
text:
Exploring Container Image Versions
A single Docker Hub repository can provide many image versions.

Versions are represented by tags.
Use : to specify a tag:

CONTAINER_IMAGE_NAME:TAG
To download Python 3.8 container image:
docker image pull python:3.8
To download Python 3.6 container image:
docker image pull python:3.6
If no tag is provided, Docker uses the default tag - latest.

title: Introduction to Docker
text:
Images from Others
Not all images are maintained by Docker.

Many are published by individuals or organizations.
Your own uploaded images follow this same model.
Examples include ContinuumIO (who develop the Anaconda Python environment) and rocker (group that builds community R container images).

Common naming pattern for owner-managed images:
OWNER/CONTAINER_IMAGE_NAME:TAG






Repositories



A Docker Hub page represents a repository.
A tag selects a specific version from that repository.

So a more precise naming pattern is:
OWNER/REPOSITORY:TAG

title: Introduction to Docker
text:
Challenge




What’s in a Name?


How would I download the Docker container image produced by the rocker group that has version 3.6.1 of R and the tidyverse installed?
Note: the container image described in this exercise is large and won’t be used later in this lesson, so you don’t actually need to pull the container image – constructing the correct docker pull command is sufficient.

title: Introduction to Docker
text:
Finding Container Images




Solution


First, search for rocker in Docker Hub. Then look for their tidyverse container image. You can look at the list of tags, or just guess that the tag is 3.6.1. Altogether, that means that the name of the container image we want to download is:
docker image pull rocker/tidyverse:3.6.1

title: Introduction to Docker
section: Finding Container Images on Docker Hub
text:
Finding Container Images on Docker Hub
Docker Hub offers many image choices.

Each image can provide a ready-to-use software environment.
Find images via Docker Hub search or software project pages.
Evaluate image quality before use.

Useful trust indicators:

The container image is updated regularly.
The container image associated with a well established company, community, or other group that is well-known.
There is a Dockerfile or other listing of what has been installed to the container image.
The container image page has documentation on how to use the container image.

Avoid images that are stale, poorly documented, or from unclear sources.

They are harder to trust.
They reduce reproducibility and reliability for research workflows.

title: Introduction to Docker
text:
Challenge




What Container Image Is Right for You?


Find a Docker container image that’s relevant to you. Take into account the suggestions above of what to look for as you evaluate options. If you’re unsuccessful in your search, or don’t know what to look for, you can use the R or Python container image we’ve already seen.
Once you find a container image, use the skills from the previous episode to download the container image and explore it.

title: Introduction to Docker
section: Why Create Your Own Container Images
text:
Why Create Your Own Container Images
There are lots of reasons why you might want to create your own Docker container image.

You can’t find a container image with all the tools you need on Docker Hub.
You want to have a container image to “archive” all the specific software versions you ran for a project.
You want to share your workflow with someone else.

title: Introduction to Docker
text:
Interactive Installation
Before building a reproducible image, test installation steps interactively.

Start an interactive container from alpine:

docker container run -it alpine sh
alpine is minimal, so tools like python3 are not preinstalled.
/# python3
sh: python3: not found
Install Python using Alpine’s package manager (apk):
/# apk add --update python3 py3-pip python3-dev
Verify the install:
/# python3 --version

title: Introduction to Docker
text:
Challenge




Searching for Help


Can you find instructions for installing R on Alpine Linux? Do they work?

title: Introduction to Docker
text:
Installing R on Alpine




Solution


A quick search should hopefully show that the way to install R on Alpine Linux is:
/# apk add R




When you exit, these interactive changes are not captured as a reproducible image.

There are commands that will “snapshot” the changes. Snapshot-style workflows are possible, but hard to reproduce.
Better approach: encode steps in a Dockerfile to create a container image.

If you haven’t already, exit out of the interactively running container.
/# exit

title: Introduction to Docker
section: Put Installation Instructions in a Dockerfile
text:
Put Installation Instructions in a Dockerfile
A Dockerfile is a plain-text recipe for building container images.
Inspect the starter Dockerfile:
cd ~/Desktop/docker-intro/basic
cat Dockerfile
FROM <EXISTING IMAGE>
RUN <INSTALL CMDS FROM SHELL>
CMD <CMD TO RUN BY DEFAULT>
What each instruction means:

FROM: which container image we’re starting with. It is the “base” container image we are going to start from.
RUN: installation commands we want to run. These are the same commands that we used interactively above.
CMD: the default command we want a container based on this container image to run, if no other command is provided.

It is recommended to provide CMD in exec-form (see the CMD section of the Dockerfile documentation for more details).
It is written as a list which contains the executable to run as its first element, optionally followed by any arguments as subsequent elements.
The list is enclosed in square brackets ([]) and its elements are double-quoted (") strings which are separated by commas.
For example, CMD ["ls", "-lF", "--color", "/etc"] would translate to ls -lF --color /etc.

title: Introduction to Docker
text:
Challenge




Exercise: Take a Guess


Do you have any ideas about what we should use to fill in the sample Dockerfile to replicate the installation we did above?

title: Introduction to Docker
text:
Solution




Solution:


Based on our experience above, edit the Dockerfile (in your text editor of choice) to look like this:
FROM alpine
RUN apk add --update python3 py3-pip python3-dev
CMD ["python3", "--version"]




This Dockerfile does three things:

starts from Alpine Linux;
installs Python 3, pip package management tool, and Python header files;
sets default runtime command to print the Python 3 version.

title: Introduction to Docker
text:
Create a New Docker Image
At this point, you only have a Dockerfile. Use docker image build to turn it into an image.
docker image build needs two key inputs:

Dockerfile location (build context)
output image name (typically USERNAME/CONTAINER_IMAGE_NAME)

Typical command format:
docker image build -t USERNAME/CONTAINER_IMAGE_NAME .
-t sets the output image name. Final . means: use current directory as build context.
Example:
docker image build -t alice/alpine-python .

title: Introduction to Docker
text:
Build Context






Build Context



The final argument to docker image build is a directory, not the Dockerfile itself.
That directory is the build context.
Docker expects the Dockerfile and any copied files to be available there.
Keep the build context small so Docker only loads what you actually need.

title: Introduction to Docker
text:
Challenge




Exercise: Review!



Think back to earlier. What command can you run to check if your container image was created successfully? (Hint: what command shows the container images on your computer?)
We didn’t specify a tag for our container image name. What tag did Docker automatically use?
What command will run a container based on the container image you’ve created? What should happen by default if you run such a container? Can you make it do something different, like print “hello world”?

title: Introduction to Docker
text:
Solution




Solution



To see your new image, run docker image ls. You should see the name of your new container image under the “REPOSITORY” heading.
In the output of docker image ls, you can see that Docker has automatically used the latest tag for our new container image.
We want to use docker container run to run a container based on a container image.

The following command should run a container and print out our default message, the version of Python:
docker container run alice/alpine-python
To run a container based on our container image and print out “Hello world” instead:
docker container run alice/alpine-python echo "Hello World"




This is a major milestone:

you combined a Linux base image with your own setup;
the same container can run consistently across macOS, Windows, Linux, and cloud systems.

title: Introduction to Docker
section: Boring but Important Notes About Installation
text:
Boring but Important Notes About Installation
Installation strategy matters. Use these guidelines:

Start from the right base image: use official Python/R images where possible.
Keep images lean: install only what you need.
Use distro-appropriate package tools: Alpine apk, Ubuntu apt/apt-get, CentOS yum, etc.
Reuse familiar installers: tools like pip or install.packages() can still be useful inside containers (if the basic scripting language is installed).
Follow upstream docs: README/install guides often provide the best Linux setup path.

A practical installation workflow:

Make a list of what you want to install.
Look for pre-existing container images.
Read through instructions for software you’ll need to install.
Try installing everything interactively in your base container – take notes!
From your interactive installation, create a Dockerfile and then try to build the container image from that.

title: Introduction to Docker
section: Share Your New Container Image on Docker Hub
text:
Share Your New Container Image on Docker Hub
To publish publicly, push your image to Docker Hub.

If the name follows the Docker Hub pattern, use docker image push:

docker image push alice/alpine-python
Use your full image name.
After pushing, confirm on https://hub.docker.com that the image appears in your account.






Logging In



You must be logged in before pushing to Docker Hub.
If docker image push fails, run docker login first.
Then retry the push command.

title: Introduction to Docker
text:
What’s in a Name? (Again)
Locally, you can name images however you like. For sharing, use Docker Hub naming format:

USERNAME/CONTAINER_IMAGE_NAME:TAG

For example, imagine someone named Alice has been working on a workflow container image and called it workflow-test on her own computer. She now wants to share it in her alice Docker Hub account with the name workflow-complete and a tag of v1.
Use docker image tag to rename/re-tag images for publishing.
Example:
docker image tag workflow-test alice/workflow-complete:v1
Then push the tagged image:

docker image push alice/workflow-complete:v1

title: Introduction to Docker
section: Working with External Files and Mounts
text:
Working with External Files and Mounts
This episode moves beyond basic container use.

Use files from your host system.
Copy files into images at build time.
Run software and scripts with those files.
Build more automated container behavior.

The examples get progressively more advanced. Most day-to-day workflows only need the first 1-2 sections.

title: Introduction to Docker
section: Using Scripts and Files from Outside the Container
text:
Using Scripts and Files from Outside the Container
In your shell, move to the sum folder and inspect its contents.
cd ~/Desktop/docker-intro/sum
ls
The folder contains:

a Dockerfile
a Python script: sum.py

Next, try running sum.py with the alpine-python image.




Running Containers


What command would we use to run Python from the alpine-python container?

title: Introduction to Docker
text:
Running Containers Solution




Solution


We can run a container from the alpine-python container image using:
docker container run alice/alpine-python
What happens? Since the Dockerfile that we built this container image from had a CMD entry that specified ["python3", "--version"], running the above command simply starts a container from the image, runs the python3 --version command and exits. You should have seen the installed version of Python printed to the terminal.
Instead, if we want to run an interactive Python terminal, we can use docker container run to override the default run command embedded within the container image. So we could run:
docker container run -it alice/alpine-python python3
The -it tells Docker to set up and interactive terminal connection to the running container, and then we’re telling Docker to run the python3 command inside the container which gives us an interactive Python interpreter prompt. (type exit() to exit!)




If we try running the container and Python script, what happens?
docker container run alice/alpine-python python3 sum.py
python3: can't open file '//sum.py': [Errno 2] No such file or directory

title: Introduction to Docker
text:
No Such File or Directory




No Such File or Directory


What does the error message mean? Why might the Python inside the container not be able to find or open our script?

title: Introduction to Docker
text:
Why the Error Happens:

Container filesystems are isolated from the host.
The container cannot see files on your computer unless you expose them.
sum.py is on the host, not inside the container.

Fix: create a mount from host to container.

${PWD} expands to your current host directory.
mount option:

`--mount type=bind,source=${PWD},target=/temp`
Interpretation:
make my current working directory (on the host computer) – the source – visible within the container that is about to be started, and inside this container, name the directory /temp – the target.






Types of Mounts



This example uses a bind mount.
Other mount types include volume and tmpfs.
We do not cover them here, but Docker documents them in more detail.

title: Introduction to Docker
section: Accessing Mounted Files in the Container
text:
Accessing Mounted Files in the Container
Let’s try running the command now:
docker container run --mount type=bind,source=${PWD},target=/temp alice/alpine-python python3 sum.py
But we get the same error!
python3: can't open file '//sum.py': [Errno 2] No such file or directory
The missing detail: use the container path to the script.

sum.py is visible at /temp/sum.py inside the container.
so run Python with that full path:

docker container run --mount type=bind,source=${PWD},target=/temp alice/alpine-python python3 /temp/sum.py
Files written in /temp from inside the container are written to your host directory and remain after the container exits.

title: Introduction to Docker
section: Other Commonly Used Docker Run Flags
text:
Other Commonly Used Docker Run Flags






Other Commonly Used Docker Run Flags


Two useful docker run flags are -w and -u.
The --workdir/-w flag sets the working directory a.k.a. runs the command being executed inside the directory specified. For example, the following code would run the pwd command in a container started from the latest ubuntu image in the /home/alice directory and print /home/alice. If the directory doesn’t exist in the image it will create it.
docker container run -w /home/alice/ ubuntu pwd
The --user/-u flag lets you specify the username you would like to run the container as. This is helpful if you’d like to write files to a mounted folder and not write them as root but rather your own user identity and group. A common example of the -u flag is --user $(id -u):$(id -g) which will fetch the current user’s ID and group and run the container as that user.

title: Introduction to Docker
text:
Challenge




Explore the Script


What happens if you use the docker container run command above and put numbers after the script name?

title: Introduction to Docker
text:
Solution




Solution


This script comes from the Python Wiki and is set to add all numbers that are passed to it as arguments.

title: Introduction to Docker
text:
Challenge




Checking the Options


Our Docker command has gotten much longer! Can you go through each piece of the Docker command above and explain what it does? How would you characterize the key components of a Docker command?

title: Introduction to Docker
text:
Solution




Solution


Here’s a breakdown of each piece of the command above

docker container run: use Docker to run a container
--mount type=bind,source=${PWD},target=/temp: connect my current working directory (${PWD}) as a folder inside the container called /temp
alice/alpine-python: name of the container image to use to run the container
python3 /temp/sum.py: what commands to run in the container

More generally, every Docker command will have the form: docker [action] [docker options] [docker container image] [command to run inside]

title: Introduction to Docker
text:
Challenge




Interactive Jobs


Try using the directory mount option but run the container interactively. Can you find the folder that’s connected to your host computer? What’s inside?

title: Introduction to Docker
text:
Solution




Solution


The docker command to run the container interactively is:
docker container run --mount type=bind,source=${PWD},target=/temp -it alice/alpine-python sh
Once inside, you should be able to navigate to the /temp folder and see that’s contents are the same as the files on your host computer:
/# cd /temp
/# ls




Mounts are useful when you want to run containerized software across many changing input files.
If you want a fixed, archived snapshot, copy files into the image itself.

title: Introduction to Docker
section: Including Your Scripts and Data Within a Container Image
text:
Including Your Scripts and Data Within a Container Image

useful for sharing a completed analysis;
useful for creating an archived, fixed version.

Assume sum.py is finalized and should be bundled in the image.
In your shell, you should still be in the sum folder in the docker-intro folder.
pwd
/Users/yourname/Desktop/docker-intro/sum
Add a COPY line to include sum.py in the image:
COPY sum.py /home
This copies sum.py from host to image filesystem.
Then build a new image:
docker image build -t alice/alpine-sum .

title: Introduction to Docker
section: The Importance of Command Order in a Dockerfile
text:
The Importance of Command Order in a Dockerfile






The Importance of Command Order in a Dockerfile


When you run docker image build it executes the build in the order specified in the Dockerfile. This order is important for rebuilding and you typically will want to put your RUN commands before your COPY commands.
Docker builds the layers of commands in order. This becomes important when you need to rebuild container images. If you change layers later in the Dockerfile and rebuild the container image, Docker doesn’t need to rebuild the earlier layers but will instead used a stored (called “cached”) version of those layers.
For example, in an instance where you wanted to copy multiply.py into the container image instead of sum.py. If the COPY line came before the RUN line, it would need to rebuild the whole image. If the COPY line came second then it would use the cached RUN layer from the previous build and then only rebuild the COPY layer.

title: Introduction to Docker
text:
Challenge




Did It Work?


Can you remember how to run a container interactively? Try that with this one. Once inside, try running the Python script.

title: Introduction to Docker
text:
Solution




Solution


You can start the container interactively like so:
docker container run -it alice/alpine-sum sh
You should be able to run the python command inside the container like this:
/# python3 /home/sum.py




COPY is useful when you want to bundle scripts or data into an image you will share or archive.
Use this guidance:

Use COPY for stable scripts and data.
Use bind mounts for files you edit often.
Check image size with docker image ls; larger images are slower to download.

title: Introduction to Docker
text:
Security Warning






Security Warning


Login credentials including passwords, tokens, secure access tokens or other secrets must never be stored in a container. If secrets are stored, they are at high risk to be found and exploited when made public.

title: Introduction to Docker
text:
Copying Alternatives






Copying Alternatives


Another trick for getting your own files into a container image is by using the RUN keyword and downloading the files from the internet. For example, if your code is in a GitHub repository, you could include this statement in your Dockerfile to download the latest version every time you build the container image:
RUN git clone https://github.com/alice/mycode
Similarly, the wget command can be used to download any file publicly available on the internet:
RUN wget ftp://ftp.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/blast/executables/blast+/2.10.0/ncbi-blast-2.10.0+-x64-linux.tar.gz
Note that the above RUN examples depend on commands (git and wget respectively) that must be available within your container: Linux distributions such as Alpine may require you to install such commands before using them within RUN statements.

title: Introduction to Docker
text:
More Fancy Dockerfile Options
You can make the image more automatic with CMD and ENTRYPOINT patterns.
Make the sum.py Script Run Automatically
FROM alpine
RUN apk add --update python3 py3-pip python3-dev
COPY sum.py /home

# Run the sum.py script as the default command
CMD ["python3", "/home/sum.py"]
Build and test it:
docker image build -t alpine-sum:v1 .
docker container run alpine-sum:v1
Doesn’t work: Running with no arguments works (e.g. sum = 0). Passing arguments this way fails:
docker container run alpine-sum:v1 10 11 12
results in
docker: Error response from daemon: OCI runtime create failed:
container_linux.go:349: starting container process caused "exec:
\"10\": executable file not found in $PATH": unknown.
Reason: 10 11 12 is interpreted as a replacement command, not arguments to the existing CMD.

title: Introduction to Docker
text:
Overriding the ENTRYPOINT






Overriding the ENTRYPOINT



Sometimes you want to bypass the image’s ENTRYPOINT.
For example, you may want an interactive shell instead of the default app.

docker container run -it alpine-sum:v2 /bin/sh
will yield
Please supply integer arguments
Override the ENTRYPOINT like this:
docker container run -it --entrypoint /bin/sh alpine-sum:v2

title: Introduction to Docker
text:
Best Practices for Writing Dockerfiles






Best Practices for Writing Dockerfiles


Take a look at Nüst et al.’s “Ten simple rules for writing Dockerfiles for reproducible data science” [1] for some great examples of best practices to use when writing Dockerfiles. The GitHub repository associated with the paper also has a set of example Dockerfiles demonstrating how the rules highlighted by the paper can be applied.
[1] Nüst D, Sochat V, Marwick B, Eglen SJ, Head T, et al. (2020) Ten simple rules for writing Dockerfiles for reproducible data science. PLOS Computational Biology 16(11): e1008316. https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pcbi.1008316

title: Introduction to Docker
text:
Practical Workflow Examples
Practical workflow examples:

Example 1: automate website deployment with GitHub Actions.
Example 2: move container-based workflows onto HPC systems.

title: Introduction to Docker
text:
Repo Structure on Main Branch
Containers can power cloud CI/CD workflows.

automate repetitive tasks;
keep build environments consistent;
deploy simple project websites automatically.

GitHub Actions
GitHub Actions automates repetitive project tasks:

Testing if your software works correctly (Continuous Integration)
Building components for distribution to your users (Continuous Deployment)
Building documentation

Why run this in the cloud?

Your software works on your computer, but you forgot to mention this one crucial package that is required for correct operation.
Your software used to work, but since the last update something broke.
Someone else contributed to your package but didn’t run the same version of the document converter: the documentation looks different now.

CI/CD helps by using a consistent, controlled environment.

GitHub Actions is configured with YAML workflow files.
Jobs run behind the scenes in container-based environments.
The example here uses a containerized Pandoc workflow to publish a simple site.

Building Content for Deployment via GitHub Pages with Pandoc
Goal: publish a simple GitHub Pages site from README.md.

the README stays the single source of truth;
Pandoc converts it to HTML;
GitHub Actions rebuilds and republishes the page automatically.

Pandoc performs the Markdown-to-HTML conversion.

title: Introduction to Docker
text:
Why Pandoc?






Why Pandoc?


Pandoc is useful here because it supports:

Supports citations (from BibTeX or CSL database)
Rendered equations (using MathJax, optionally numbered)
Code highlighting
Highly customizable




Workflow:

start with a repository containing README.md on main;
convert it to index.html using Pandoc;
publish the built files to gh-pages.

title: Introduction to Docker
text:
GitHub Actions: Example Repository
Example repository: jhidding/readme-pages

title: Introduction to Docker
text:
Rendered GitHub Pages Output
Pandoc converts the README into a simple static site.

title: Introduction to Docker
text:
gh-pages Branch Contents
The gh-pages branch holds the published site.

title: Introduction to Docker
text:
Why Only index.html and .nojekyll?
Only index.html and .nojekyll are needed.

.nojekyll tells GitHub Pages to skip Jekyll processing.
Pages then serves the branch content directly.

title: Introduction to Docker
text:
Create a GitHub Project
Create a GitHub project with a short README.md:

Log in to GitHub.
Create a new repository.
Clone it locally (GitHub shows the exact command).

git clone <your-repo-url>
cd <repo-name>

title: Introduction to Docker
text:
Verify Generated HTML Output
Check the Pandoc container first:
docker container run pandoc/core --version
Convert README.md inside the container:
docker container run --mount type=bind,source=${PWD},target=/tmp pandoc/core /tmp/README.md
<h1 id="readme-pages">readme-pages</h1>
<p>Example for generating GitHub.io pages from Readme with Pandoc.</p>
The mount exposes your repository as /tmp inside the container, so Pandoc can read and write files on your machine.
To create a complete HTML document:

Add --standalone flag for full document structure
Redirect output to save as index.html in build/ directory

mkdir -p build
docker container run --mount type=bind,source=${PWD},target=/tmp pandoc/core /tmp/README.md --standalone --output=/tmp/build/index.html
[WARNING] This document format requires a nonempty <title> element.
  Defaulting to 'README' as the title.
  To specify a title, use 'title' in metadata or --metadata title="...".
To suppress the warning, add this header to README.md (Or pass --metadata title="..." on the command line):
---
title: Hello, Pandoc
---

title: Introduction to Docker
section: Using Pandoc to Create a Website

title: Introduction to Docker
text:
Create the Workflow and Trigger
GitHub Actions can rebuild and publish on each commit.
In GitHub, navigate to your project and create a new workflow:

Click Actions tab
Skip workflow templates

Select set up a workflow yourself
Configure in YAML format

Start with the workflow name:
name: Deploy pages
Define trigger conditions, for example push to main:
on:
  push:
    branches:
      - main

title: Introduction to Docker
text:
Define Build Job Steps
Then define the job steps.
jobs:
  # a free machine-readable name for this job
  deploy:
    # Set permissions for this specific job to enable it to write to the gh-pages branch
    permissions:
      contents: write
    # specify the base operating system
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      # fetch the contents of the repository
      - name: Checkout repo content
        uses: actions/checkout@v2
      - name: Prepare build environment
        run: |   # multiple Bash commands follow
          mkdir -p build
          touch build/.nojekyll

title: Introduction to Docker
text:
Add Pandoc and Deploy Steps
Add the containerized Pandoc step:
      - name: Run pandoc
        # Always specify a version!
        uses: docker://pandoc/core:2.12
        with:
          args: >-  # multi-line argument
            --standalone
            --output=build/index.html
            README.md
      - name: Deploy on github pages
        # Use a third-party plugin to upload the content
        uses: JamesIves/github-pages-deploy-action@4.1.0
        with:
          branch: gh-pages
          folder: build
Key differences in GitHub Actions:

No --mount flag needed; files placed automatically in correct location
Last step uploads build directory to gh-pages branch

Enable GitHub Pages:

In repository Settings, scroll to GitHub Pages
Select root folder of gh-pages branch
Deploy completes in seconds

title: Introduction to Docker
section: Using Containers on an HPC Cluster
text:
Using Containers on an HPC Cluster
Containers are also useful on shared research infrastructure:

build and test locally;
run at scale on a cluster later;
keep the software environment consistent across both places.

One catch: many HPC systems do not allow Docker itself for security reasons. Instead, they often use Singularity, Apptainer, or Shifter, which can still consume Docker-built images.






Future Workshop: Apptainer Deep Dive


Detailed Apptainer usage will be covered in the next workshop.



Apptainer is similar to Docker, but typically uses .sif image files and is often better suited to shared HPC systems.
Apptainer vs Singularity

Apptainer is the actively maintained continuation used on many modern HPC systems.
Singularity appears in many existing tutorials and cluster docs.
In practice, commands are very similar, so older Singularity references are still useful.

title: Introduction to Docker
text:
Apptainer Command Line Interface




Apptainer Command Line Interface


Like we did with Docker, try to work out what commands Apptainer has. Which one do you think is the equivalent of docker run?

title: Introduction to Docker
text:
Solution




Note


apptainer run behaves similarly to docker run, but as we will see, the arguments are somewhat different.




Running Docker Images with Apptainer
Apptainer uses its own image format, so Docker images are typically pulled/converted first:
apptainer pull docker://alice/alpine-python
This creates alpine_python.sif in your working directory. Run it with:
apptainer run alpine_python.sif
Apptainer Exec
To run a custom command, use apptainer exec:
apptainer exec alpine_python.sif python -c 'print(1 + 1)'

title: Introduction to Docker
section: Bringing It Together: The Reproducibility Focus
text:
Bringing It Together: The Reproducibility Focus
So far, we focused mostly on Docker mechanics. This section focuses on reproducibility in research workflows.






Work in Progress…



Reproducibility practices for containers are still evolving.
The principles here are useful now, but best practice will continue to change.

title: Introduction to Docker
text:
Reproducibility
Reproducibility means:

someone else (or future you) can rerun your computational work later
results are as close as possible to the original
exact same hardware is not required

Why this matters:

research outputs increasingly depend on software
software stacks are complex and dependency-heavy
others need to verify and build on published work

title: Introduction to Docker
text:
Why Containers Help Reproducibility
Containers are useful because they let you:

run the same workflow across Windows, macOS, and Linux
capture the exact software versions and dependencies
preserve older software environments for legacy workflows
optionally bundle key reference data with the environment
archive a reusable snapshot that others can inspect and rerun

title: Introduction to Docker
text:
Sharing Images
Docker Hub is useful for day-to-day sharing and collaboration.
But it is not ideal for long-term research archiving because:

free accounts have a limit on how long a container image will be hosted if it is not updated
it does not support adding persistent identifiers to container images
it is easy to overwrite tagged container images with newer versions by mistake.

title: Introduction to Docker
section: Archiving and Persistently Identifying Container Images Using Zenodo
text:
Archiving and Persistently Identifying Container Images Using Zenodo
For publications, archive images in an immutable, citable form. Zenodo is one option.
Zenodo accepts tar archives. Export an image with docker image save:
docker image save alice/alpine-python:v1 -o alpine-python.tar
If needed, compress before upload:
gzip alpine-python.tar
Note: Zenodo has upload size limits (for example, 50 GB per upload at the time of writing), so compression (gzip/zip) may be required.

title: Introduction to Docker
text:
Why Archive with Zenodo?
After creating the archive, deposit it on Zenodo to:

create a long-term archive snapshot that others can download and reuse
mint a persistent DOI (Digital Object Identifier) for citation in publications
support attribution and credit for your computational work

You will also provide metadata about the image and related work.
Zenodo is one option; some institutions provide equivalent archive/DOI services.

title: Introduction to Docker
text:
Reproducibility Good Practice

use images to capture the computational environment for your work
choose container granularity based on your workflow and domain norms
document what you did and why in both image docs and project docs
when publishing, archive the exact image and cite its DOI
use tags so older image versions remain accessible

title: Introduction to Docker
text:
Reproducibility Good Practice (Important Caveat)

a built and archived image gives a durable software snapshot
a Dockerfile is lightweight and useful for rebuilding later
if you only keep a Dockerfile, pin versions explicitly
unpinned dependencies can change and break reproducibility over time

title: Introduction to Docker
text:
Container Granularity
An important design choice is container granularity.
Two extremes are:

create one large image with all tools for the full workflow
create many small images, each for one command or workflow step

Most real workflows use a middle ground between these extremes.

title: Introduction to Docker
text:
Positives and Negatives




Positives and Negatives


What are the advantages and disadvantages of the two approaches to container granularity for research workflows described above? Think about this and write a few bullet points for advantages and disadvantages for each approach in the course Etherpad.

title: Introduction to Docker
text:
Solution




Solution


This is not an exhaustive list but some of the advantages and disadvantages could be:
Single Large Container Image

Advantages:

Simpler to document
Full set of requirements packaged in one place
Potentially easier to maintain (though could be opposite if working with large, distributed group)

Disadvantages:

Could get very large in size, making it more difficult to distribute

Could use Docker multi-stage build to reduce size

May end up with same dependency issues within the container image from different software requirements
Potentially more complex to test
Less re-useable for different, but related, work


Multiple Smaller Container Images

Advantages:

Individual components can be re-used for different, but related, work
Individual parts are smaller in size making them easier to distribute
Avoid dependency issues between different pieces of software
Easier to test

Disadvantage:

More difficult to document
Potentially more difficult to maintain (though could be easier if working with large, distributed group)
May end up with dependency issues between component container images if they get out of sync

title: Introduction to Docker
text:
Container Workflow Challenges




Next Steps with Containers


Now that we’re at the end of the lesson material, take a moment to reflect on what you’ve learned, how it applies to you, and what to do next.

In your own notes, write down or diagram your understanding of Docker containers and container images: concepts, commands, and how they work.
In the workshop’s shared notes document, write down how you think you might use containers in your daily work. If there’s something you want to try doing with containers right away, what is a next step after this workshop to make that happen?

title: Introduction to Docker
text:
Thank You
Questions and discussion.

title: Episode 1: Introducing Containers
text:
Questions




What are containers, and why might they be useful to me?









Objectives




Show how software depending on other software leads to configuration management problems.
Identify the problems that software installation can pose for research.
Explain the advantages of containerization.
Explain how using containers can solve software configuration problems












What’s Your Experience?




Think about software frustrations in your research workflow.
Compare with a neighbour and list common pain points.
Focus on installation, versions, portability, and reproducibility.










software is unavailable on your preferred OS;
installation pulls in many nested dependencies;
only some version combinations work;
colleagues get different results on different systems;
reproducing an older setup becomes difficult.





research software depends on many layers;
one missing or incompatible piece can break the stack;
this situation is often called dependency hell.







What Is a Software Dependency?




A dependency is software that another tool needs in order to work.
Example: an application may depend on a library to talk to a database.









you may be unable to use a required tool at all;
results become harder to reproduce with confidence;
moving work to new systems or collaborators becomes difficult;
others may be unable to validate or extend your work.

Containers help by packaging software, dependencies, and access to resources in a more consistent, reproducible way.



Think of a container as a self-contained working environment inside your existing computer:

isolated from your main software setup;
cheaper and lighter than a separate machine;
portable across systems with the same container engine.

More concretely, Docker Inc use the following definition of a container:

A container is a standard unit of software that packages up code and all its dependencies so the application runs reliably from one computing environment to another.

https://www.docker.com/resources/what-container/
The shipping-container analogy is useful:

containers keep related things together;
they standardize transport and reuse;
the same container can be run on any compatible host system.











Virtualization



Virtualization means running an isolated computing environment on a main (host) computer.

Containers are an example of virtualization
Virtual machines (VMs) include a full operating system and are heavier.
Containers are lighter and usually share the host’s Linux kernel.
Containers still provide an isolated filesystem and runtime for applications.






Docker is a platform for building and running containers.



Key distinction:

a container image is the recipe or template;
a container is the running instance created from that image;
images persist, containers are usually transient.

The cookie-cutter analogy works well: one cutter creates many cookies of the same shape.




Containers help because the environment becomes explicit, portable, and easier to recreate.



documentation – there is a clear record of what software and software dependencies were used, from bottom to top.
portability – the container can be used on any computer that has Docker installed – it doesn’t matter whether the computer is Mac, Windows or Linux-based.
reproducibility – you can use the exact same software and environment on your computer and on other resources (like a large-scale computing cluster).
configurability – containers can be sized to take advantage of more resources (memory, CPU, etc.) on large systems (clusters) or less, depending on the circumstances.






Run a specific tool locally without a difficult install.
Test software while avoiding dependency hell.
Build an image from a Dockerfile for a defined research setup.
Share that image with collaborators or on cloud and HPC systems.
Archive images so you can rerun work with the same software later.

title: Introduction to Docker
text:
This hands-on workshop introduces Docker as a tool for creating reproducible and portable computational environments. Participants will learn how to build, manage, and share containerised applications, with a focus on practical workflows relevant to research computing. The sessions will cover core Docker concepts, working with images and containers, writing Dockerfiles, managing data within containers, and best practices for reproducibility. By the end of the workshop, participants will be able to create and run their own Docker containers for research workflows.
This workshop is primarily aimed at using Docker on your own laptop, though the skills covered are applicable to cloud and HPC environments as well.

title: Introduction to Docker
text:
Prerequisites

You should have basic familiarity with using a command shell, and the lesson text will at times request that you “open a shell window”, with an assumption that you know what this means.

Under Linux or macOS it is assumed that you will access a bash shell (usually the default), using your Terminal application.
Under Windows, Powershell and Git Bash should allow you to use the Unix instructions. We will also try to give command variants for Windows cmd.exe.

The lessons will sometimes request that you use a text editor to create or edit files in particular directories. It is assumed that you either have an editor that you know how to use that runs within the working directory of your shell window (e.g. nano), or that if you use a graphical editor, that you can use it to read and write files into the working directory of your shell. We recommend Visual Studio Code (VS Code) as an IDE and text editor for this workshop.


Website accounts to create
Please seek help at the start of the lesson if you have not been able to establish a website account on:

The Docker Hub. We will use the Docker Hub to download pre-built container images, and for you to upload and download container images that you create, as explained in the relevant lesson episodes.



Files to download
Download the docker-intro.zip file. Move the downloaded file to your Desktop and unzip it. It should unzip to a folder called docker-intro.


Software to install
Docker’s installation experience has steadily improved, however situations will arise in which installing Docker on your computer may not be straightforward unless you have a large amount of technical experience. Workshops try to have helpers on hand that have worked their way through the install process, but do be prepared for some troubleshooting.
In most cases, you will need to have administrator rights on the computer in order to install the Docker software. If you are using a computer managed by your organisation and do not have administrator rights, you may be able to get your organisation’s IT staff to install Docker for you. Alternatively your IT support staff may be able to give you remote access to a server that can run Docker commands.
Please try to install the appropriate software from the list below depending on the operating system that your computer is running. Do let the workshop organisers know as early as possible if you are unable to install Docker using these instructions, as there may be other options available.


Microsoft Windows

You must have admin rights to run Docker! Some parts of the lesson will work without running as admin but if you are unable to Run as administrator on your machine some elements of this workshop might not work as described.
Ideally, you will be able to install the Docker Desktop software, following the Docker website’s documentation. Note that the instructions for installing Docker Desktop on Windows 10 Home Edition are different from other versions of Windows 10.
Note that the above installation instructions highlight a minimum version or “build” that is required to be able to install Docker on your Windows 10 system. See Which version of Windows operating system am I running? for details of how to find out which version/build of Windows 10 you have.
If you are unable to follow the above instructions to install Docker Desktop on your Windows system, the final release of the deprecated Docker Toolbox version of Docker for Windows can be downloaded from the releases page of the Docker Toolbox GitHub repository. (Download the .exe file for the Windows installer). Please note that this final release of Docker Toolbox includes an old version of Docker and you are strongly advised not to attempt to use this for any production use. It will, however, enable you to follow along with the lesson material.






Warning: Git Bash



If you are using Git Bash as your terminal on Windows then you should be aware that you may run into issues running some of the commands in this lesson as Git Bash will automatically re-write any paths you specify at the command line into Windows versions of the paths and this will confuse the Docker container you are trying to use. For example, if you enter the command:
docker run alpine cat /etc/os-release
Git Bash will change the /etc/os-release path to C:\etc\os-release\ before passing the command to the Docker container and the container will report an error. If you want to use Git Bash then you can request that this path translation does not take place by adding an extra / to the start of the path. i.e. the command would become:
docker run alpine cat //etc/os-release
This should suppress the path translation functionality in Git Bash.




Apple macOS
Ideally, you will be able to install the Docker Desktop software, following the Docker website’s documentation. The current version of the Docker Desktop software requires macOS version 10.14 (Mojave) or later.
If you already use Homebrew or MacPorts to manage your software, and would prefer to use those tools rather than Docker’s installer, you can do so. For Homebrew, you can run the command brew install --cask docker. Note that you still need to run the Docker graphical user interface once to complete the initial setup, after which time the command line functionality of Docker will become available. The Homebrew install of Docker also requires a minimum macOS version of 10.14. The MacPorts Docker port should support older, as well as the most recent, operating system versions (see the port details), but note that we have not recently tested the Docker installation process via MacPorts.



Linux

There are too many varieties of Linux to give precise instructions here, but hopefully you can locate documentation for getting Docker installed on your Linux distribution. It may already be installed. If it is not already installed on your system, the Install Docker Engine page provides an overview of supported Linux distributions and pointers to relevant installation information. Alternatively, see:

Docker Engine on CentOS
Docker Engine on Debian
Docker Engine on Fedora
Docker Engine on Ubuntu




Verify Installation
To quickly check if the Docker and client and server are working run the following command in a new terminal or ssh session:
$ docker version
Client:
 Version:           20.10.2
 API version:       1.41
 Go version:        go1.13.8
 Git commit:        20.10.2-0ubuntu2
 Built:             Tue Mar  2 05:52:27 2021
 OS/Arch:           linux/arm64
 Context:           default
 Experimental:      true

Server:
 Engine:
  Version:          20.10.2
  API version:      1.41 (minimum version 1.12)
  Go version:       go1.13.8
  Git commit:       20.10.2-0ubuntu2
  Built:            Tue Mar  2 05:45:16 2021
  OS/Arch:          linux/arm64
  Experimental:     false
 containerd:
  Version:          1.4.4-0ubuntu1
  GitCommit:        
 runc:
  Version:          1.0.0~rc95-0ubuntu1~21.04.1
  GitCommit:        
 docker-init:
  Version:          0.19.0
  GitCommit:        
The above output shows a successful installation and will vary based on your system. The important part is that the “Client” and the “Server” parts are both working and returns information. It is beyond the scope of this document to debug installation problems but common errors include the user not belonging to the docker group and forgetting to start a new terminal or ssh session.


A quick tutorial on copy/pasting file contents from episodes of the lesson
Let’s say you want to copy text off the lesson website and paste it into a file named myfile in the current working directory of a shell window. This can be achieved in many ways, depending on your computer’s operating system, but routes I have found work for me:

macOS and Linux: you are likely to have the nano editor installed, which provides you with a very straightforward way to create such a file, just run nano myfile, then paste text into the shell window, and press control+x to exit: you will be prompted whether you want to save changes to the file, and you can type y to say “yes”.
Microsoft Windows running cmd.exe shells:

del myfile to remove myfile if it already existed;
copy con myfile to mean what’s typed in your shell window is copied into myfile;
paste the text you want within myfile into the shell window;
type control+z and then press enter to finish copying content into myfile and return to your shell;
you can run the command type myfile to check the content of that file, as a double-check.

Microsoft Windows running PowerShell:

The cmd.exe method probably works, but another is to paste your file contents into a so-called “here-string” between @' and '@ as in this example that follows (the “>” is the prompt indicator):
> @'
Some hypothetical
file content that is

split over many

lines.
'@ | Set-Content myfile -encoding ascii
